حد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حد (hadd), from Arabic حَدّ (ḥadd).

حدود.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حُدُود (hudūd), from Arabic حُدُود (ḥudūd), plural of حَدّ (ḥadd).

حدیث.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَدِیث (hadīs), from Arabic حَدِيث (ḥadīṯ).

حذف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حذف, from Arabic حَذْف (ḥaḏf)

senses.g: slight fever, feverish, to have a temperature

حرارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَرَارَت (harārat), borrowed from Arabic حَرَارَة (ḥarāra).

حراست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حراست (hirāsat), ultimately from Arabic حِراسَة (ḥirāsa).

حرام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَرَام (harām), from Arabic حَرَام (ḥarām)

حرف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَرْف (harf), borrowed from Arabic حَرْف (ḥarf), from حَرَفَ (ḥarafa).

حرف عطف.e: Borrowed from Arabic حَرْف عَطْف (ḥarf ʕaṭf).

حرم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حرم (haram), from Arabic حَرَم (ḥaram).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حرم (haram), from Arabic حَرَم (ḥaram).

senses.g: reverence, respect, honour, dignity; 'a thing that should be sacred or inviolable,' (hence) honour, reputation, character; chastity

حرمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حرمت (hurmat), from Arabic حُرْمَة (ḥurma).

senses.g: Huramzai (a city in Pishin district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

حرمزئی.e: Named after Huramzai, founder of the town.

حزقیال.e: Borrowed from Arabic حِزْقِيَال (ḥizqiyāl), Biblical Hebrew יחזקאל (y'ḥez'qel, “God strengthens”).

senses.g: bill (a list of items needed to paid for)

حساب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِسَاب (hisāb), borrowed from Arabic حِسَاب (ḥisāb). Compare Bengali হিসাব (hiśab), Assamese হিচাপ (hisap).

حسرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَسْرَت (hasrat), from Arabic حَسْرَة (ḥasra).

حسین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حسین, itself from Arabic حَسِين (ḥasīn), from the root ح س ن (ḥ s n).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حسین, itself from Arabic حَسِين (ḥasīn), from the root ح س ن (ḥ s n).

senses.g: Husayn, a son of Ali and brother of Hasan; a martyr, and Shi'a Imam.
a male given name, Hussain or Husain, from Arabic

حسین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حسین (husayn), itself from Arabic حُسَيْن (ḥusayn).

حشرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَشَره (hašara), from Arabic حَشَرة (ḥašara).

ex.u:
وہ نُبُوَّت ہَے مُسَلْمان کے لِیے بَرْگِ حَشِیش
نُبُوَّت میں نَہِیں قُوت و شَوکَت کا پَیام
vo nubuvvat hai musalmān ke liye barg-i haśīś
nubuvvat mẽ nahī̃ qūt o śaukat kā payām
ex.e:
That kind of prophethood is hashish for the Muslim
In which there is not the message of power and energy!

حشیش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَشِیش (hašīš), from Arabic حَشِيش (ḥašīš).

حصہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِصَّه (hissa), from Arabic حِصَّة (ḥiṣṣa).

senses.g: Hadrat (a title applied to any great man, his Majesty, Highness, Excellency, Eminence, Worship, or Holiness, etc. (when so used respectfully instead of a pronoun or name the gender of the person to whom it has reference is adopted)

حضرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حضرت (hazrat), from Arabic حَضْرَة (ḥaḍra).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حضرت (hazrat), from Arabic حَضْرَة (ḥaḍra).

senses.g: Hazratganj (a neighbourhood of Lucknow, Uttar Pradesh, India)

حضرت گنج.e: Named after Amjad Ali Shah, from حَضْرَت (hazrat) + ـگَنْج (-ganj).

حضور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حضور (huzūr), from Arabic حُضُور (ḥuḍūr).

حفاظت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِفاظَت, from Arabic حِفَاظ (ḥifāẓ). Related to حافظ (hāfiz).

senses.g: a right (ie. a privilege or claim by law etc.)
reward (ie. a right that is earned)

حق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَقّ (haqq), borrowed from Arabic حَقّ (ḥaqq). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi حق (ḥq /⁠haq(q)⁠/).

حقوق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حُقُوق (huqūq), from Arabic حُقُوق (ḥuqūq). First attested in c. 1707 as Middle Hindi حقوق (ḥqvq /⁠huqūq⁠/).

senses.g: oblique plural of حَق (haq, “truth; right; share”)

حقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حقه (huqqa), from Arabic حُقَّة (ḥuqqa)

حقیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَقِیر (haqīr), from Arabic حَقِير (ḥaqīr).

حقیقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَقِیقَت (haqīqat), from Arabic حَقِيقَة (ḥaqīqa). Related to حَق (haq).

senses.g: narrative, narration, history, story, tale, romance

حکایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِکَایَت (hikāyat), from Arabic حِكَايَة (ḥikāya).

حکم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حُکْم (hukm), borrowed from Arabic حُكْم (ḥukm).

حکمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حکمت (hikmat), from Arabic حِكْمَة (ḥikma).

حکومت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حُکُومَت (hukūmat), borrowed from Arabic حُكُومَة (ḥukūma), from حَكَمَ (ḥakama).

senses.g: one of the 99 names of Allah

حکیم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حکیم (hakīm), from Arabic حَكِيم (ḥakīm). Compare Punjabi ਹਕੀਮ (hakīm) / حکیم (hakīm), Gujarati હકીમ (hakīm).

حلال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حلال (halāl), from Arabic حَلال (ḥalāl).

senses.g: Aleppo (the capital city of Aleppo governorate, Syria)

حلقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَلْقَه (halqa), from Arabic حَلْقَة (ḥalqa).

senses.g: oblique singular/ vocative singular/ direct plural of حَلْقَہ (halqē)

حلم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِلْم (hilm), from Arabic حِلْم (ḥilm).

senses.g: A type of South Asian dessert.
haleem, (a stew-like dish traditionally consisting of wheat, mutton, spices, and other ingredients, popular in the Middle East, Central Asia, and the Indian subcontinent)
demeanour or more strictly - the countenance, face
the description of a person (ie. to identify or recognise a person)
jewellery, ornament, decoration (particularly of gold, silver, jewels etc.)

حلیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حلیه (hiliya), itself from Arabic حِلِيَة (ḥiliya).

حمار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حِمَار (himār), from Arabic حِمَار (ḥimār).

senses.g: Hamas (a Palestinian Islamist militant group and political organization)

حماقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حماقت (himāqat), from Arabic حَمَاقَة (ḥamāqa).

حمایت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حمایت (himāyat), from Arabic حِمَايَة (ḥimāya).

ex.u:
حمد و ثنا اُسی کو جو ذات جاودانی
ہمسر نہیں ہے اُس کا کوئی نہ کوئی ثانی
ex.r:
hamd o sanā usī ko jo zāt-i jāvidānī
hamsar nahī̃ hai us kā koī na koī sānī
ex.e:
All praise belongs to the Being, who is Eternal, Everlasting;
He has no associate, none is His equal.

ex.u: حمد اور نعت لکھنا ہماری شاعری کی ایک ممتاز اور اہم روایت ہے۔
ex.e: The composition of hymns and naats is a prominent and important tradition, part of our poetry.

حمد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حمد (hamd), taken from Arabic حَمْد (ḥamd).

حمرا.e: Borrowed from Arabic حَمْرَاء (ḥamrāʔ), feminine singular of أَحْمَر (ʔaḥmar).

حمل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حمل (haml), from Arabic حَمْل (ḥaml).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حمل (hamal), from Arabic حَمَل (ḥamal).

senses.g: oblique plural of حَمْلَہ (hamla, “attack; invasion; assault”)

ex.u: رہنما پر حملے کی مذمت کی گئی۔
ex.r: rahnumā par hamle kī mazammat kī gaī.
ex.e: The attack on the leader was condemned.

senses.g: inflection of حَمْلَہ (hamla, “attack; invasion; assault”): — direct plural
inflection of حَمْلَہ (hamla, “attack; invasion; assault”): — oblique/vocative singular
henna (the plant (Lawsonia inermis) and the dye prepared from it)

حنا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حنا (hinā), from Arabic حِنَّاء (ḥinnāʔ).

senses.g: a companion of any other Prophet
disciple (of a teacher or sage etc.)

حواری.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حواری (havārī), itself from Arabic حَوَارِيّ (ḥawāriyy), from Ge'ez ሐዋርያ (ḥäwarəya, “traveller; messenger, envoy; apostle”).

senses.g: houri, a beautiful virgin-nymph that resides in Paradise

حور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حُور (hūr, “houri”), from Arabic حُور (ḥūr).

senses.g: morale, confidence, willpower, strength of spirit

حوصلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَوْصَلَه (hawsala, “crop, maw; patience, forbearance; courage”), from Arabic حَوْصَلَة (ḥawṣala, “craw, bladder”).

senses.g: reservoir, cistern, pond, pool, tank, vat, trough, basin of a fountain, a fountain

حویلی.e: From Classical Persian حوالی (havālī, “vicinity, outskirts”), from Arabic حَوَالَيْ (ḥawālay, “around”), probably due to the suburban setting, or alternatively from Arabic حَوَالِي (ḥawālī, “private space”), referring to the nature or function, popularized in Mughal India.

حیا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حیا (ḥayā), itself from Arabic حَيَاء (ḥayāʔ). First attested as Middle Hindi حیا (ḥayā)

حیات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیَات (hayāt), from Arabic حَيَاة (ḥayāh). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi حیات (ḥyat /⁠hayāt⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیَات (hayāt), from Arabic حَيَاة (ḥayāh). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi حیات (ḥyat /⁠hayāt⁠/).

senses.g: Hyderabad (the capital city, the largest city in Telangana, India)
Hyderabad (a former princely state in British India)

حیران.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیرَان (hayrān), borrowed from Arabic حَيْرَان (ḥayrān).

senses.g: surprise, puzzle, astonishment (ie. something unexpected, a wonder, amazement, neither positive nor negative)

حیرانگی.e: From حَیرَان (hairān, “surprising, surprised”, adjective) + ـگِی (-gī). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi حیرانگی (ḥyrangy /⁠hairāngī⁠/).

حیرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیرَت (hayrat), borrowed from Arabic حَيْرَة (ḥayra). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi حیرت (ḥyrt /⁠hairat⁠/).

حیرت انگیز.e: From Classical Persian حیرت (“surprising”) + Classical Persian انگیز (“inspiring”).

senses.g: Haifa (a port city in northern Israel)

حیفا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیفَا (hayfā), borrowed from Arabic حَيْفَا (ḥayfā), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew חיפה (ḥēp̄ɔ̂).

حیوان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian حَیْوَان (haywān), borrowed from Arabic حَيَوَان (ḥayawān).

senses.g: The tenth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ح and followed by د.

خاتمہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَاتِمَه (xātima), borrowed from Arabic خَاتِمَة (ḵātima).

خار.e: From Classical Persian خار (xār, “thorn”), from Middle Persian hʾl (xār, “thorn”).

senses.g: Kharan (a city in Kharan district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

خارج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خارج (xārij), from Arabic خَارِج (ḵārij).

خارجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خارجه (xārija), from Arabic خَارِجَة (ḵārija).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خارجه (xārija), from Arabic خَارِجَة (ḵārija).

خارجی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خارجی (xārijī), from Arabic خَارِجِيّ (ḵārijiyy).

خاص.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَاصّ (xāss), borrowed from Arabic خَاصّ (ḵāṣṣ), from خَصَّ (ḵaṣṣa).

senses.g: especially, for the most part, specially

خاطر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خاطر (xātir), from Arabic خاطِر (ḵāṭir).

senses.g: first-person singular subjective personal pronoun: I, I humbly, this servant

خاکسار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَاکْسَار (xāksār, literally “earth-like”). By surface analysis, خَاک (xāk) + ـسَار (-sār).

خاکنائے.e: Probably coined after آبنای (âbnây), from a compound of خاک (xâk, “earth, dirt”) + نای (nây, “lane, throat, pipe”).

خال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خال (xāl), from Arabic خال (ḵāl).

ex.u: مخلوق میں خالق کا نظر آیا جس کو اس دیکھنے والے نے خدا کو دیکھا
ex.r: maxlūq mẽ xāliq kā nazar āyā jis ko us dekhne vāle ne xudā ko dekhā
ex.e: Who has seen the creator in the creation, that observer has seen God

senses.g: husband of mother’s sister (maternal uncle) – masculine of خَالَہ (xāla)

خالو.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خالُو, from Arabic خَال (ḵāl).

خالہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَالَه (xāla), from Arabic خَالَة (ḵāla).

خالی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَالِی (xālī), from Arabic خَالٍ (ḵālin).

خام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خام (xām) from Proto-Iranian *HaHmáh, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *h₂eh₃mós (“raw, uncooked”). Doublet of آم (ām).

senses.g: to be quiet, to be silent

خاموش ہونا.e: From خاموش (xāmoš, “silent”) + ہونا (honā, “to be”), literally “to be silent”.

خاموشی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خاموشی (xāmōšī). By surface analysis, خاموش (xāmoś) + ـی (-ī).

senses.g: reed, especially when cut and dried for use
pen, a writing instrument originally made of reed
kind of graving tool, such as a stonecutter's chisel
pencil, or other instrument for writing or ruling

خامہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَامَه (xāma, “reed; pen”).

senses.g: Khanpur (a city in Rahim Yar Khan district, Punjab, Pakistan)
a convent for Sufi recluses; khanaqah
a convent, monastery, a religious establishment for holy men

خانقاہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic خَانَقَاه (ḵānaqāh), from Classical Persian خانگاه (xānagāh), from خانه (xāna) + ـگاه (-gāh).

senses.g: a lady, a woman of rank, a princess; title of the wife of a ḵẖān
Khanozai (a city in Karezat district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خانَه, from Middle Persian 𐭡𐭩𐭲𐭠 (BYTA /⁠xānag⁠/).

senses.g: Khanewal (a city in Khanewal district, Punjab, Pakistan)

خانیوال.e: Named after Sangar Khan, who built a castle in the area.

خبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خبر (xabar), from Arabic خَبَر (ḵabar).

senses.g: Khaplu (a city in Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan, located in the Ghanche district)

ختم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian ختم (xatm), itself from Arabic خَتْم (ḵatm).

ختنہ.e: From Classical Persian ختنه (xatna), from Arabic خَتْنَة (ḵatna).

خجیر.e: From Classical Persian خُجیر (xujīr, “beautiful; pleasant, agreeable”).

ex.u: مَخْلُوق میں خالِق نَظَر آیا ہو جِس کو اُس دیکھْنے والے نے خُدا کو دیکھا ہَے۔
ex.r: maxlūq mẽ xāliq nazar āyā ho jis ko us dekhne vāle ne xudā ko dekhā hai.
ex.e: Who has seen the creator in the creation, that observer has seen God.

خدا حافظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُدَا حَافِظ (xudā hāfiz), Contraction of خدا حافظت باشد (xudā hāfizat bāšad, “may God be your protector”)

خدا نخواستہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُدَا نَخْوَاسْتَه (xudā naxwāsta).

senses.g: alternative form of خدا حافظ (xudā hāfiz)
service; employment (as part of work; job etc.)
presence; obedience (ie. in the presence of someone)

خدمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خِدْمَت (xidmat), borrowed from Arabic خِدْمَة (ḵidma), from خَدَمَ (ḵadama). First attested in c. 1603 as Middle Hindi خدمت (xdmt /⁠xidmat⁠/).

خدیو.e: From Classical Persian خدیو (xadēw, “lord; king; mighty ruler”).

خراب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خراب (xarāb), from Arabic خَرَاب (ḵarāb).

خراٹا.e: Onomatopoeic from خَر (xar) + اٹا (āṭā). Compare Arabic خَر (ḵar, “snore, gurgle”).

ex.u: وہ خِراماں خِراماں چَل رَہی تھی۔.
ex.r: vo xirāmā̃ xirāmā̃ cal rahī thī..

senses.g: melon: usually musk melon, Cucumis melo; sometimes watermelon

ex.u: خربوزے کو دیکھ کر خربوزہ رنگ بدلتا ہے
ex.r: xarbūze ko dekh kar xarbūzā raṅg badaltā hai
ex.e: People try to be like others. (idiom)

خربوزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خربوزه (xarbûza, “melon”) (modern خربزه (xarboze)), from Middle Persian hlbyck (xarbīzag).

ex.u: مَیں ا ِتْنے زْیادَہ کھانے کا خَرْچ کَیسے اُٹھا پاؤں گا؟
ex.r: ma͠i itne zyāda khāne ka xarc kese uṭhā pāũ ga
ex.e: How will I be able to bear the expenses for this much food?

خرچ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خرچ (xarč), alternative form of خرج (xarj), from Arabic خَرْج (ḵarj). Compare Assamese খৰচ (khoros), Bengali খরচ (khoroc), Gujarati ખર્ચ (kharc), Kannada ಖರ್ಚು (kharcu), Odia ଖରଚ (kharaca), Punjabi ਖਰਚ (kharac), Telugu కర్చు (karcu).

خردبین.e: From خرد (xurd, “small”) + بین (-bīn, “seeing”), both elements from Classical Persian.

خردل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَرْدَل (xardal), from Arabic خَرْدَل (ḵardal).

خرس.e: PIE word *h₂ŕ̥tḱos From Classical Persian خرس (xirs, “bear”).

خریدنا.e: Urdu verb set خَرِیدْنا (xarīdnā) خَرِیدْوَانَا (xarīdvānā) From خَرِید (xarīd, “purchase”) + ـنَا (-nā).

ex.e: Have the clothes bought by her.

خریدوانا.e: Causative form of خَرِیدْنا (xarīdnā) with stem augmented by وا (vā)

خزانہ.e: First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi خزانہ (xizāna), borrowed from Classical Persian خِزَانَه (xizāna), from Arabic خِزَانَة (ḵizāna).

خزاں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خزان (xazān), probably from Bactrian. First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi خزاں (xzaṉ).

خستہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَسْتَه (xasta). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi خستہ (xsth /⁠xasta⁠/).

senses.g: a khasra: record of all land ownership in a region

خسرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَسَرَه (xasara), from Arabic خَسَرَة (ḵasara).

خشت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خِشت (xišt, “brick”), doublet of اِیْنٹ (ī̃ṭ). Ultimately derived from the BMAC substrate. Cognate with Punjabi اِٹّ (īṭṭ), Pashto خښته (ḵẖaś̱ẖtaʿh), Khowar اݰٹو (ušṭu), Palula اݜٹو (iṣṭū́),

خشک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خُشْک (xušk), from Proto-Iranian *Hhúškah, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *Hsúškas, from Proto-Indo-European *h₂sews- + *-kos. Doublet of سُوکھا (sūkhā, “dried up”) and شُشْک (śuśk). Cognate with Punjabi خُشْک (xuśk).

خصم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خصم (xasm, “adversary; master, husband”), from Arabic خَصْم (ḵaṣm). Cognate with Punjabi کَھسَم (khasam), Haryanvi खसम (khasam).
Borrowed from Classical Persian خصم (xasm, “adversary; master, husband”), from Arabic خَصْم (ḵaṣm). Cognate with Punjabi کَھسَم (khasam), Haryanvi खसम (khasam).

خصوصا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خصوصا (xusūsan), from Arabic خُصُوصًا (ḵuṣūṣan, “especially”).

senses.g: Khuzdar (a city in Khuzdar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

خط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَطّ (xatt), borrowed from Arabic خَطّ (ḵaṭṭ), from خَطَّ (ḵaṭṭa).

senses.g: a mistake; error; fault, guilt, omission, failure, default, neglect

خطا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَطَا (xatā), from Arabic خَطَأ (ḵaṭaʔ). First attested in c. 1578 as Middle Hindi خطا (xt̤a /⁠xatā⁠/).

خطبہ.e: From Arabic خُطْبَة (ḵuṭba) (through Classical Persian خطبه (xut̤ba)).

خطرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَطْرَه (xatra), borrowed from Arabic خَطَر (ḵaṭar).

senses.g: inflection of خَطْرہ (“risk, danger”): — direct plural
inflection of خَطْرہ (“risk, danger”): — oblique/vocative singular
piece of land; plot of land

خطہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خِطَّه (xitta, “territory; ground; country”), from Arabic خِطَّة (ḵiṭṭa).

senses.g: an orator, preacher (one who delivers a sermon)
representative (one who speaks for a community; group)

خطیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خطیب (xat̤īb), itself from Arabic خَطِيب (ḵaṭīb).

خطیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خطیه (xatīya), from Arabic خَطِيئَة (ḵaṭīʔa).

خفیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خفیه (xufya), from Arabic خُفْيَة (ḵufya).

خلا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خلاء (xalā'), from Arabic خَلَاء (ḵalāʔ).

خلافت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خلافت (xilāfat), from Arabic خِلَافَة (ḵilāfa).

خلفا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خلفا (xolafâ), from Arabic خُلَفَاء (ḵulafāʔ).

خلق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خَلْق (xalq), borrowed from Arabic خَلْق (ḵalq).

خلقت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خِلْقَت (xilqat), from Arabic خِلْقَة (ḵilqa).

خلل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خلل (xalal), from Arabic خَلَل (ḵalal).

senses.g: of or pertaining to a cell; cellular
the most gifted wrestler (after the teacher)
a Muslim cook, tailor, barber etc.

خلیفہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian خلیفه (xalifa), from Arabic خَلِيفَة (ḵalīfa).

senses.g: an aggregate of five poetical pieces; a stanza of five lines
